title: W8 B Multidim Concave Functions
body:
Mathematical Economics

     Juan Carlos Carbajal

  School of Economics UNSW–Sydney



  Week 8B    —    Term 1, 2026




                                    1/9
 Concave and Convex Functions in Rn




                                     2/9
                    Concave and Convex Functions


 Definition 1

   Let S ⊆ Rn be a convex set and f : S → R a real-valued function.
    (a) f is said to be a concave function if for all x, y ∈ S and all
        α ∈ (0, 1) one has
                                   
                 f αx + (1 − α)y       ≥ αf (x) + (1 − α)f (y).

    (b) f is said to be a convex function if for all x, y ∈ S and all
        α ∈ (0, 1) one has
                                   
                 f αx + (1 − α)y       ≤ αf (x) + (1 − α)f (y).



Remark: the notion of concave or convex functions only makes sense under
a convex domain.




                                                                           3/9
                                Examples



Example 1

  (a) The Euclidean distance to z, dz : Rn → R+ defined by
      dz (x) = ∥x − z∥, is a convex function.

 (b) The Cobb-Douglas function u(x1 , x2 ) = xα 1 α2           2
                                              1 x2 defined on R++
      is concave, provided α1 , α2 ≥ 0 and α1 + α2 ≤ 1.

  (c) The Leontieff function v(x1 , x2 ) = min{x1 , x2 } defined on R2+ is
      concave.

 (d) The linear function w(x1 , x2 ) = c1 x2 + c2 x2 defined on R2 is
      both concave and convex.




                                                                             4/9
                 Checking Concavity and Convexity



Sometimes it is simple to verify concavity or convexity of a function directly
via definitions.

 Lemma 1

   The Euclidean norm ∥ · ∥ in Rn is a convex function.


Proof   It follows directly from the triangle inequality. For any α ∈ (0, 1),

           ∥α x + (1 − α) y∥ ≤ α∥x∥ + (1 − α)∥y∥.


Sometimes it is easier to take an alternative route.




                                                                                 5/9
                 Playing with Concavity and Convexity

 Proposition 1

    Let S ⊆ Rn be a convex set and f : S → R, g : S → R be two concave
    functions. Then
     (a) The function f + g is concave.
    (b) If c ≥ 0, then c f is concave.
     (c) If c ≤ 0, then c f is convex.


Proof
(a) Let x, y ∈ S and α ∈ (0, 1). Then
                                                            
    (f + g) αx + (1 − α)y = f αx + (1 − α)y + g αx + (1 − α)y

                               ≥ αf (x) + (1 − α)f (y) + αg(x) + (1 − α)g(y)

                               = α(f + g)(x) + (1 − α)(f + g)(y).

Parts (b) and (c) are left as exercises.



                                                                               6/9
              Playing with Concavity and Convexity


 Proposition 2

   Let f : S → R and g : I → R, where S ⊆ Rn and I ⊆ R are convex
   sets, and the range f (S) ⊆ I. If f and g are concave, and g is weakly
   increasing, then the composite function h = g ◦ f is concave on S.


Proof    Let x, y ∈ S and λ ∈ (0, 1). Then
                                           
        h λx + (1 − λ)y = g f λx + (1 − λ)y
                                                    
                           ≥ g λf (x) + (1 − λ)f (y)




                                                                            7/9
              Playing with Concavity and Convexity


 Proposition 2

   Let f : S → R and g : I → R, where S ⊆ Rn and I ⊆ R are convex
   sets, and the range f (S) ⊆ I. If f and g are concave, and g is weakly
   increasing, then the composite function h = g ◦ f is concave on S.


Proof    Let x, y ∈ S and λ ∈ (0, 1). Then
                                           
        h λx + (1 − λ)y = g f λx + (1 − λ)y
                                                    
                           ≥ g λf (x) + (1 − λ)f (y)
                                                        
                           ≥ λ g f (x) + (1 − λ) g f (y)

                           = λ h(x) + (1 − λ) h(y).




                                                                            7/9
                Checking Concavity and Convexity



Let f (x1 , x2 ) = xα   α2              2
                    1 x2 be defined on R++ , with αj ≥ 0 and α1 + α2 ≤ 1.
                      1


The Hessian matrix of f is
              2           2
                                                   α (α −1)                 
                  ∂ f (x)     ∂ f (x)                   1    1     α1 α2
      2           ∂x1 ∂x1     ∂x1 ∂x2                       x2
                                                             1
                                                                   x1 x2
    D f (x) =                             = f (x)                          .
                  ∂ 2 f (x)   ∂ 2 f (x)                 α1 α2    α2 (α2 −1)
                  ∂x2 ∂x1     ∂x2 ∂x2                   x1 x2        x2
                                                                      1




                                                                                   8/9
                  Checking Concavity and Convexity



Let f (x1 , x2 ) = xα   α2              2
                    1 x2 be defined on R++ , with αj ≥ 0 and α1 + α2 ≤ 1.
                      1


The Hessian matrix of f is
              2           2
                                                    α (α −1)                 
                   ∂ f (x)     ∂ f (x)                   1    1     α1 α2
      2            ∂x1 ∂x1     ∂x1 ∂x2                       x2
                                                              1
                                                                    x1 x2
    D f (x) =                              = f (x)                          .
                   ∂ 2 f (x)   ∂ 2 f (x)                 α1 α2    α2 (α2 −1)
                   ∂x2 ∂x1     ∂x2 ∂x2                   x1 x2        x2
                                                                       1



The ‘sign’ of the Hessian matrix is determined by whether this is positive
semidefinite or negative semidefinite.

In this case, note α1 (αx12 −1) ≤ 0, and
                        1

     α1 (α1 − 1) α2 (α2 − 1)   α1 α2 α1 α2   α1 α2
                             −             = 2 2 (1 − α1 − α2 ) ≥ 0.
          x21         x22      x1 x2 x1 x2   x1 x2

D2 f (x) is negative semi-definite.



                                                                                    8/9
            Concavity/Convexity and Differentiability

 Proposition 3

   Let S ⊆ Rn be open and convex and f : S → R be twice continuously
   differentiable. Then
   (a) f is concave on S if and only if D2 f (x) is negative semi-definite,
        for all x ∈ S;
   (b) f is convex on S if and only if D2 f (x) is positive semi-definite,
        for all x ∈ S;
   (c) if D2 f (x) is negative definite for all x ∈ S, then f is strictly
        concave on S;
   (d) if D2 f (x) is positive definite for all x ∈ S, then f is strictly
        convex on S.


Proof   Omitted
